chunks.title: CSCE 221 Syllabus / Course Information / Catalog Description / Special Course Designation / Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
College of Engineering
Computer Science and Engineering
CSCE 221 Syllabus
Section 200 (43689)
Data Structures and Algorithms
Spring 2026 - College Station

Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LEC
Meeting Days: MWF
Start Time: 08:00AM
End Time: 08:50AM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location: ZACH 244
Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LAB
Meeting Days: MW
Start Time: 12:40PM
End Time: 1:30PM
Start Date: 01/12/2026
End Date: 05/05/2026
Meeting Location: ZACH 582
Credit Hours: 4

Data Structures and Algorithms. (3-2). Credit 4. Specification and implementation
of basic abstract data types and their associated algorithms including stacks,
queues, lists, sorting and selection, searching, graphs, and hashing; performance
tradeoffs of different implementations and asymptotic analysis of running time and
memory usage; includes the execution of student programs written in C++.
Prerequisite: Grade C or better in CSCE 120 or CSCE 121; grade of C or better in
CSCE 222 or ECEN 222, or concurrent enrollment.

None

• All homework assignments will be announced in class and posted on
Canvas. See the link to the course calendar on Canvas for deadlines.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade C or better in CSCE 120 or CSCE 121; grade of
C or better in CSCE 222/ECEN 222 or ECEN 222/CSCE 222, or concurrent
enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Design and implement a variety of data structures that support efficient
data manipulation using the C++ programming language.
• Apply Big-O asymptotic notation to analyze algorithmic efficiency and
select appropriate algorithms based on runtime and memory considerations.
• Analyze a contemporary or recent development to an algorithm covered in
the course.
• Evaluate how the modern approach improves upon earlier methods,
highlighting the specific advantages, performance gains, or conceptual
innovations it introduces.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials / Additional Instructional Materials / Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Data Structures and Algorithm Analysis in C++
ISBN: 978-0132847377
Authors: Mark A. Weiss
Publisher: Pearson
Publication Date: June 13, 2013
Edition: 4th edition

This Material is: Optional
A free website devoted to teaching you how to program in modern C++
URL for Resource: http://learncpp.com

• The written part of homework assignments or programming reports, and the
Cover Page should be typed preferably using LaTeX or LaTeX-based
apps, for instance Overleaf (an online LaTeX text editor). The homework in
PDF format should be submitted to Gradescope.
• All programming assignments should be implemented in C++, compiled
and run on a computer (preferably using Linux), and transferred to
Gradescope for grading.

chunks.title: Grading Policy / Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
Grading
Criteria
| Score | Grade |
| :---- | :---- |
| 90-100 | A |
| 80-89.9 | B |
| 70-79.9 | C |
| 60-69.9 | D |
| 0-59.9 | F |

Grade Weights
| Component | Weight |
| :-------------------- | :----- |
| Homework Assignments | 9% |
| Programming Assignments | 24% |
| Lab Activities | 10% |
| Quizzes | 12% |
| Midterm Exam 1 | 15% |
| Midterm Exam 2 | 15% |
| Final Exam | 15% |

• Your final grade will be determined based on written homework,
programming assignments, lab activities, quizzes, and exams.
• The assignment handout will provide points distribution.
• All grade appeals must be made no later than one week after the grade
is posted.
• Quizzes are over material covered during the lectures and/or assigned
reading from the course material.
• The Instructor and/or TAs can request oral exam to check student's
knowledge over material covered by test, homework, assignment or lecture
and lab activities.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy / Course Specific Late Work Policy / Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

• Make-up exams and quizzes will only be given with documented University-
approved excuses, see University Regulations. The student Rule 07 explains
policies and excused absences.
• A homework assignment will be accepted up to 1 day late with a 5%
penalty. Once solutions have been discussed or handed out the assignments
will not be accepted.
• Please discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor.

• The students lab attendance is required and will be taken on regular basis. 2
bonus points will be added to your final score if you have a perfect lab
attendance, or 1 point if you have only one lab absence.
• The lecture attendance will be taken by pop quizzes. The students are
responsible to learn all material covered in class, read the assigned text from
the textbook and do homework assignments.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the
students themselves, although discussion with TAs, PTs and Instructor is allowed.
Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any portion of an
assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write
assignments and/or using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT. (Adapted from
University of Massachusetts Amherst)

chunks.title: Course Summary
chunks.content:
CSCE 221 | Data Structures and Algorithms | Spring 2026
Instructor: Teresa Leyk | teresa19@tamu.edu
Meets: LEC: MWF 08:00AM-08:50AM | ZACH 244; LAB: MW 12:40PM-1:30PM | ZACH 582
Topics: Dijkstra Algorithms, Prim's Algorithm, Kruskal Algorithm, Huffman Trees, BFS, DFS, Digraph Algorithms, Shortest Path Algorithms, Minimum Spanning Trees, 2-4 Trees, Skip Lists, AVL Trees, Read-Black Trees, Binary Search Trees, Heap, Priority Queues, Linked Lists, Dequeues, Stack ADT, Queue ADT, Disjoint Set ADT, Hashing, Linear Sort Algorithms, Recursive Algorithms, Divide-and-Conquer Algorithms, Iterating and Master Methods, Search Algorithms, Sort Algorithms, Algorithms - Best, Worst, Average Cases, Lower Bound Theorem, Amortized Analysis, Stack and Queue Applications, Parser, Big-O asymptotic notation, asymptotic analysis, algorithmic efficiency, performance tradeoffs, runtime and memory usage, data manipulation, specification and implementation, contemporary algorithm analysis, evaluating modern approaches, computational thinking, programming skills, abstract data types, data structures, algorithms, graphs, stacks, queues, lists
Tools: C++, LaTeX, LaTeX-based apps, Overleaf, Gradescope, Linux, Canvas, Campuswire
Prerequisites: Grade C or better in CSCE 120 or CSCE 121; grade of C or better in CSCE 222 or ECEN 222 or ECEN 222/CSCE 222, or concurrent enrollment.
